chunk_summary: This section provides case-based decision pathways for managing dysglycemia and dyslipidemia in children with metabolic risk, focusing on diagnostic interpretation, lifestyle interventions, and treatment strategies based on clinical guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic challenges and management of discordant glycemia in children.
Learn to interpret and address insulin-resistance dyslipidemia patterns in pediatric patients.
Develop treatment plans for youth-onset diabetes and early kidney risk in children with T2DM.
key_facts: HbA1c can be falsely elevated in cases of microcytic anemia; confirm prediabetes with fasting glucose or OGTT.
Insulin-resistance dyslipidemia is characterized by fasting triglycerides >350 mg/dL and low HDL-C, often linked to high sugar intake.
Diabetes-range glycemia with HbA1c ≥8.5% and no ketosis warrants basal insulin and metformin initiation.
Nephropathy screening in T2DM starts at diagnosis; confirm albumin/creatinine ratio >30 mg/g with two of three first-morning samples.
Lifestyle interventions, such as eliminating sugary drinks and adding protein-rich meals, are critical in managing pediatric dyslipidemia.
reference_quote: "Iron deficiency can falsely elevate HbA1c; treat anemia and confirm with repeat fasting glucose and, if still discordant, OGTT." — NGSP, Factors that interfere with HbA1c test results
document_key_topics: Early kidney risk in pediatric T2DM
